name: Lio
founder: Vladimir Keil (CEO), Lukas Heinzmann (CTO), Till Wagner (COO)
overview: World's first multi-agent AI procurement workforce; handles purchase requests end-to-end (vendor research, negotiation, approvals, tracking).
investors: a16z (led); SV Angels, Harry Stebbings, YC
strengths: Strong positioning in procurement / procure-to-pay / intake-to-pay
Agentic AI layer that executes full procurement workflows on top of existing P2P/ERP systems
Backed by a16z (led); SV Angels, Harry Stebbings, YC
weaknesses: Heavy dependence on ERP integration quality
Likely enterprise implementation complexity
Many offerings still early-stage or scaling
opportunities: Growing enterprise demand for agentic AI workflows
ERP modernization and automation budgets increasing
Potential expansion into adjacent operational workflows
threats: Competition from SAP, Oracle, Workday, and Microsoft ecosystems
Enterprise security and governance concerns
Rapid commoditization of AI workflow automation
strategicNotes: Multi-agent system reduces procurement workload 85%; operates across ERPs/inboxes/contracts.
evidenceNote: Capabilities, workflow overlap and positioning are inferred from public category signals — verify before use in deals.

battlecard.sells: World's first multi-agent AI procurement workforce; handles purchase requests end-to-end (vendor research, negotiation, approvals, tracking).

Cross-system orchestration above the stack (not a point tool)
Persistent workflow state + exception reasoning with human approvals
battlecard.objection: “We already use a tool for this workflow — why add Vyasa?”
battlecard.response: Vyasa is the reasoning + orchestration layer above your stack; it coordinates across the very tools you already run rather than replacing one of them.
discovery: Which systems does this process span today (ERP, CRM, inbox, contracts)?
How are exceptions and approvals handled when the workflow breaks?
Do you need orchestration across departments, or a single point fix?
